justification: All three input sources (Bird, MJ, Pippen) are represented in the brief. All 3 ACs present with exact values ($4.50, $5.00, $0.01). Pippen's operational notes explicitly included: high-traffic context (500 req/sec), JSDoc requirement marked as non-optional in both open_questions and Notes for Shaq, unit tests only. The $0.01 floor is labeled as a domain rule with testable assertion and appears in terminology_alignment as a domain invariant. Quantity boundary >= 10 preserved exactly in domain_rules and AC1/AC2.

observations.text: All three agent sources clearly synthesized — Bird's domain rules, MJ's architecture decisions, and Pippen's operational context all present
AC3 (price floor) fully preserved with exact $0.01 value and clear given/when/then
Quantity boundary >= 10 preserved exactly in domain_rules with testable assertions showing both sides of the boundary
JSDoc requirement explicitly called out as non-optional in Notes for Shaq: 'The JSDoc requirement from Pippen is non-optional'
terminology_alignment section bridges domain language to implementation (floored -> Math.max), which aids Shaq's implementation
JSDoc appears in open_questions rather than as a firm requirement in the main brief body — could be misread as uncertain, though the Notes for Shaq section corrects this
No invented information — all content traceable to the three agent inputs
Notes for Shaq section adds useful implementation ordering guidance (floor clamp after discount) without inventing new requirements

agent_output_excerpt:
```yaml
handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement a pure TypeScript function `recalculatePrice(quantity, basePrice)`
    that applies bulk discount pricing logic. This function will be called inline
    from a high-traffic pricing endpoint (~500 req/sec peak). No I/O, no external
    dependencies — pure computation only. A unit test suite covering all three
    acceptance criteria is required.

  domain_rules:
    - rule: Final price = base price minus applicable discounts
